content.assessment: The supplied international-comparison evidence base contains no compensatory-funding or non-structural equity comparator; all strong/moderate comparator findings in this evidence set concern structural tracking reforms (Finland, Poland) or the tracking mechanism itself (Hanushek & Woessmann, Bavaria). This is a genuine comparability gap for this lens: no cross-national mechanism-outcome pair exists here to assess transferability against. What comparative evidence is available speaks against the premise implicit in this proposal—that equity gaps can be substantially narrowed without altering track structure—since the cross-national econometric literature attributes inequality increases specifically to the existence of early tracking, and Hungary's between-school variance (third-highest in OECD) is structurally linked to its tracking regime rather than to funding or instructional-time levels per se. This does not mean the proposal is wrong, only that this lens's evidence base offers no comparator mechanism test and instead offers evidence framing the untouched structural variable (tracking) as the primary driver of the inequality this proposal seeks to address indirectly.
opportunities: Could serve as a complement to a structural reform (as in Finland's bundling of delay with systemic teacher-training and curriculum change) rather than a substitute, if paired with future structural measures
strengths: Does not carry the Polish-style transition-shock risk associated with structural reform, since no structural change is proposed
threats: Without any comparator-validated mechanism-outcome pairing, there is no international evidence basis to predict whether this class of reform can offset the structurally-rooted variance patterns documented for Hungary
weaknesses: No comparator case in the evidence set tests non-structural compensatory equity packages against early-tracking-driven inequality, so this lens cannot establish comparability or outcome relevance for the proposed mechanism

Cross-national evidence attributes Hungary's between-school variance primarily to tracking structure itself, suggesting that a reform which leaves tracking untouched addresses a downstream symptom rather than the structural variable comparators identify as central
